md: Differentiation runs in one direction: function → derivative. **Antidifferentiation** runs the other way: given $f$, find a function $F$ whose derivative is $f$. This is **integration**, the second great branch of calculus.
**Example.** $F(x) = x^2$ is an antiderivative of $f(x) = 2x$, because $F'(x) = 2x$.
But also $F(x) = x^2 + 7$ works. And $x^2 - 100$. Any constant added still gives the same derivative.
**General fact:** if $F$ is one antiderivative of $f$, then $F(x) + C$ is also an antiderivative for any constant $C$. These are ALL of them.
Read it as 'the integral of $f$ with respect to $x$'. The result is the most general antiderivative — including the **constant of integration $C$**.
**Always include $+ C$** in indefinite integrals. Forgetting it is one of the most common errors.
**Reverse the power rule:** add 1 to the exponent, divide by the new exponent.
**Special case** $n = -1$: $\int x^{-1}\,dx = \int \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx = \ln|x| + C$. (The power rule fails at $n = -1$ because of division by 0.)

blocks.tex: \int \sin x\,dx = -\cos x + C \qquad \int \cos x\,dx = \sin x + C
\int \sec^2 x\,dx = \tan x + C \qquad \int \sec x\tan x\,dx = \sec x + C

blocks.title: Antiderivatives have minus signs flipped
blocks.body: $\int \sin x\,dx = -\cos x + C$ (not $+\cos x$). $\int \cos x\,dx = +\sin x + C$. The negative is on the cosine result, not the sine. Differentiate to verify: $\frac{d}{dx}(-\cos x) = -(-\sin x) = \sin x$ ✓.

md: Often a problem gives extra info that pins down $C$.
*A ball is thrown upward from height 6 ft with initial velocity 20 ft/sec. Acceleration is $-32$ ft/sec². Find position $s(t)$.*
